Metadata.Case_Number: Civil Appeal No 21 of 2024 (Suit No 418 of 2018)
Metadata.Coram: Woo Bih Li JAD, Debbie Ong Siew Ling JAD and See Kee Oon JAD
Metadata.Tribunal_Court: Appellate Division of the High Court of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: J. G. Jewelry Pte Ltd

Facts.Text: J. G. Jewelry Pte Ltd (JGJ) is a Singapore-incorporated company in the business of trading jewellery and precious stones and was incorporated on 31 March 2015 with an issued share capital of US$100 comprising 100 ordinary shares.
On 15 April 2015, Shaileshkumar Manubhai Khunt, Michael Bernard Kriss and David Miles Kriss were appointed as directors of JGJ; a nominee resident director, Ng Chee Wooi Michael, was also appointed.

In May 2015, JGJ opened a bank account with Israel Discount Bank in New York (IDBNY); the signatories were Amit Shah, Ashish Shah, Jim Goldsborough and the Kriss Brothers, any one of whom could authorise payments.

In May 2016, a total of US$700,000 was injected into JGJ and described in remittance forms as 'Capital Introduction'; funds were transferred from S Goldi (Asia) Limited to Shailesh, and then from Shailesh to JGJ in three tranches.

In September 2016, JGJ engaged KPMG India to carry out an accounting exercise described as an accounting reconciliation; KPMG withdrew in October 2017 citing difficulties in obtaining required support and data.
The parties agree that their business collaboration involving JGJ, SRK Entities and JDM Entities was officially terminated on 31 August 2017.

In May 2017, JGJ's daily trial balances (general ledger) for 2016 and January–August 2017 were emailed by Pradeep Navale to JGJ's auditors AT Adler, copying Vikas Padhya, Vibhor Jain and Shashikant Mahadev Aienkar; the ledgers recorded supplies from SRK and The Jewelry Company as 'Purchases' alongside other third-party suppliers.

On 5 July 2017, JDM CFO Jim Goldsborough emailed tax adviser Jeffrey Sacks stating that SRK was one of JGJ's large suppliers 'selling to JGJ' and discussed a proposed comfort letter from SRK to address JGJ's going concern issues.

On 5 October 2017, SRK CFO Rajiv Shah circulated 2017 balance sheet spreadsheets including a line showing JGJ's payables to SRK Entities of about US$25.62m and a comment describing US$700,000 as 'Loan by SRK Group in JGJ at Singapore'.

On 28 December 2017, TJC Jewelry, Inc commenced proceedings in New York (Case Index No. 657583/2017) against JDM Entities to recover diamonds and jewellery inventory allegedly stored in JDM's New York vault.

On 27 March 2018, the JDM Entities and JGJ commenced proceedings in New York (Case Index No. 651469/2018) against SRK Entities and Ashish Shah, including claims for recovery of alleged excess capital contributions and half of JGJ's profits.

On 22 January 2018, the Kriss Brothers and Ng signed a directors' resolution (First Resolution) authorising commencement and management of legal proceedings on behalf of JGJ against TJC Jewelry, Inc to recover US$3,733,503.43; Shailesh did not sign.

On 15 February 2018, the Kriss Brothers and Ng signed a directors' resolution (Second Resolution) authorising commencement and management of legal proceedings against SRK and The Jewelry Company to recover alleged excessive charges; Shailesh did not sign.

On 6 March 2018, the Kriss Brothers and Ng signed a directors' resolution (Third Resolution) approving JGJ's 2016 revised financial statements and resolving that the second AGM be convened; Shailesh did not sign.
On 4 April 2018, notice was given that JGJ's AGM would be held on 19 April 2018 to adopt the 2016 revised financial statements; Shailesh objected and did not attend.

On 30 April 2018, Jim Goldsborough sent Shailesh a further resolution (Fourth Resolution) seeking to appoint solicitors for JGJ and authorise the Kriss Brothers to act for JGJ in Suit 418; Shailesh did not sign.

On 23 April 2018, SRK commenced Suit No 418 of 2018 in Singapore against JGJ claiming US$23,400,456.25 under 205 unpaid invoices issued between 2 September 2016 and 2 August 2017 (the '23M Invoices').
On 6 November 2020, The Jewelry Company filed a counterclaim in Suit 418 against JGJ for US$2,211,077.91 under invoices issued between 8 October 2016 and 1 August 2017 (the '2.2M Invoices').

On 18 January 2024, the High Court delivered judgment in Suit 418, holding that JGJ was subject of dispute in unjust enrichment to pay SRK and The Jewelry Company reasonable compensation for goods supplied and dismissing JGJ's counterclaims.

On 27 February 2024, the High Court ordered JGJ to pay SRK interest at 5.33% per annum on US$17,538,999.49 from 23 April 2018 to 18 January 2024 and to pay The Jewelry Company interest at 5.33% per annum on US$2,027,846.91 from 6 November 2020 to 18 January 2024.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether JGJ is contractually subject of dispute to pay SRK and The Jewelry Company the full amounts claimed under the 23M Invoices and 2.2M Invoices, or whether the goods were supplied as non-payable capital contributions under a joint venture arrangement such that JGJ has no or reduced liability and is entitled to counter-restitution for payments made under earlier invoices.
Party_Details.Rule: Contractual liability to pay arises where goods are supplied under invoices evidencing sales, unless parties agreed otherwise; a party asserting that invoices do not attract payment liability bears the evidential burden to prove a different agreed basis. A company not party to a joint venture agreement cannot enforce its terms absent ratification, estoppel, or statutory third-party rights. Alternative defences based on conditions precedent (such as accounting reconciliations or termination settlement exercises) require proof that liability was agreed to be contingent on those events.
Party_Details.Application: JGJ argued that SRK and the JDM Entities entered into a binding joint venture agreement in early 2015 under which all goods supplied by SRK Entities and JDM Entities pursuant to the collaboration were to be treated as capital contributions to the joint venture, invoiced only for tax and transfer pricing purposes, and not intended to create payment liability on JGJ. It contended that the 23M Invoices and 2.2M Invoices were issued at manipulated prices inconsistent with an agreed cost-plus pricing methodology and that SRK failed to conduct an agreed accounting reconciliation and a termination settlement exercise, which JGJ said were conditions precedent to any payment obligation. JGJ further argued that even if the joint venture agreement was unenforceable, SRK's unjust enrichment claim should fail because JGJ did not receive the goods or profits, had changed its position, and was entitled to counter-restitution of US$42,994,312.66 paid under the 42M Invoices and other benefits allegedly received by SRK Entities. On appeal, JGJ maintained that the High Court erred in treating the joint venture agreement as unenforceable, in finding that JGJ was enriched, and in limiting counter-restitution to the excess over reasonable compensation.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The appellate court held that the existence of invoices addressed to JGJ for goods supplied by SRK and The Jewelry Company created a prima facie contractual obligation on JGJ to pay for those goods. The court found that JGJ's own daily trial balances recorded the supplies from SRK and The Jewelry Company as 'Purchases' alongside other third-party suppliers, and that payments made under the 42M Invoices were matched to specific invoices, supporting the characterisation of sales. The court considered contemporaneous correspondence, including JGJ's request for a comfort letter acknowledging liabilities to SRK and the Moses & Singer letter asserting set-off rather than denying liability, and concluded that JGJ initially accepted that amounts were payable. The court accepted that there was some form of joint venture between SRK Entities and JDM Entities but held that JGJ was not a party to the joint venture agreement and could not enforce its terms; attempts to rely on implied contracts, estoppel by convention, or the Contracts (Rights of Third Parties) Act were rejected. The court found that JGJ failed to prove any agreed 'Capital Term' under which goods supplied to JGJ were non-payable capital contributions, noting the absence of such a term in the 13 January 2015 memorandum, draft JV agreements, KPMG's MIS memorandum, and other contemporaneous documents. It held that even if goods were treated as 'capital' for internal joint venture accounting, this did not negate JGJ's liability to pay its suppliers. The court rejected JGJ's alternative defences that liability was contingent on an accounting reconciliation or a termination settlement exercise, finding insufficient evidence that such events were agreed conditions precedent and observing that such an arrangement would allow JGJ to postpone payment indefinitely. On quantum, the court accepted evidence that the parties agreed to invoice goods on a sell-minus basis and held that JGJ was liable for the full invoiced amounts: US$23,400,456.25 to SRK under the 23M Invoices and US$2,211,077.91 to The Jewelry Company under the 2.2M Invoices. It therefore treated the High Court's unjust enrichment analysis as unnecessary and set aside the counter-restitution award to JGJ in respect of the 42M Invoices. The court affirmed pre-judgment interest at 5.33% per annum from the dates of writ and counterclaim to the date of judgment and adjusted the costs orders below only in relation to the percentage reduction applied to the SRK Parties' costs.

Metadata.Case_Number: Civil Appeal No 22 of 2024 (Suit No 418 of 2018)
Metadata.Coram: Woo Bih Li JAD, Debbie Ong Siew Ling JAD and See Kee Oon JAD
Metadata.Tribunal_Court: Appellate Division of the High Court of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Shree Ramkrishna Exports Pvt Ltd
Party_Details.Law_Firm: Audent Chambers LLC; Oon & Bazul LLP

Facts.Text: Shree Ramkrishna Exports Pvt Ltd (SRK) is an India-incorporated company in the business of manufacturing and trading jewellery and precious stones, including diamonds, with a sales office in Mumbai and a jewellery manufacturing factory in Sachin, India.

In December 2014, the Kriss Brothers met Rahul Dholakia in Mumbai; according to Michael Kriss, Rahul proposed a joint venture between JDM Entities and SRK-related entities, and the parties shook hands and said 'mazal', while Rahul described the discussions as exploring a closer partnership.

On 13 January 2015, Amit Shah emailed the Kriss Brothers a document titled 'Joint Venutre Between JDM Group and The Jewelry Co. (SRK Group)' (the '13 January Memo') outlining proposed collaboration guidelines.

From 1 April 2015, SRK and its related entities supplied diamonds and jewellery to US-based affiliates of SRK and JDM, with the goods invoiced to JGJ while being physically delivered to JDM Entities and TJC Jewelry, Inc in the US.
Between 15 September 2015 and 14 April 2017, SRK issued invoices to JGJ totalling US$42,994,312.66 (the '42M Invoices'); JGJ made payments from its IDBNY account to SRK in respect of these invoices.
Between 2 September 2016 and 2 August 2017, SRK issued 958 invoices to JGJ for goods supplied; 205 invoices remained unpaid, totalling US$23,400,456.25 (the '23M Invoices').
On 1 March 2018, SRK sent a letter of demand to JGJ seeking payment of the sums due under the 23M Invoices plus interest, describing them as 'repayment of dues of SRK'.

On 11 March 2018, JGJ's US counsel Moses & Singer LLP responded to SRK's demand, asserting that any amounts due from JGJ to SRK were offset and exceeded by amounts allegedly due from SRK to JGJ, and referring to an intended accounting reconciliation and profit sharing.
On 23 April 2018, SRK commenced Suit No 418 of 2018 in the Singapore High Court against JGJ, claiming US$23,400,456.25 under the 23M Invoices for goods sold and delivered.

SRK pleaded that the parties operated under a business arrangement (BA) whereby SRK sold goods to JGJ, JGJ sold to JDM Entities, and JGJ operated an IDBNY account from which payments to SRK were made after agreed credit periods.

SRK denied that a binding joint venture agreement with the terms alleged by JGJ was concluded and stated that negotiations on a potential joint venture were ongoing while goods were sold under the BA.
In the alternative to its contractual claim, SRK pleaded that JGJ was unjustly enriched at SRK's expense by receiving the benefit of goods without paying for them and sought reasonable compensation.

In the High Court judgment on 18 January 2024, SRK's primary contractual claim on the 23M Invoices was dismissed; SRK succeeded on an unjust enrichment claim and was awarded US$17,538,999.49 after set-off against JGJ's counter-restitution on the 42M Invoices.

On 27 February 2024, the High Court ordered JGJ to pay SRK interest at 5.33% per annum on US$17,538,999.49 from 23 April 2018 to 18 January 2024 and fixed SRK Parties' costs at S$487,500 after a 25% reduction.
SRK and The Jewelry Company appealed in Civil Appeal No 22 of 2024 against dismissal of their primary contractual claims, the counter-restitution award to JGJ, and aspects of costs and interest.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether SRK is entitled to succeed on its primary contractual claim against JGJ for US$23,400,456.25 under the 23M Invoices, or only on an alternative unjust enrichment basis with a reduced quantum, and whether JGJ is entitled to counter-restitution for payments made under the 42M Invoices.
Party_Details.Rule: Where goods are supplied and invoiced to a company, the invoices and accounting records are prima facie evidence of a contract of sale and an obligation to pay the invoiced amounts. A party asserting that invoices were not intended to create payment liability must displace this presumption with cogent evidence of a different agreed basis. A company that is not a party to a joint venture agreement cannot rely on its terms to avoid liability. Counter-restitution in unjust enrichment is available only where payments were made on a basis that has failed and are not due under a valid contract.
Party_Details.Application: SRK argued that it sold goods to JGJ under 958 invoices, that JGJ consistently paid earlier invoices from its IDBNY account, and that the 205 unpaid invoices totalling US$23.4m remained due. It relied on the structure of the arrangement, the issuance of invoices to JGJ, and JGJ's own ledgers recording the supplies as purchases and payables. SRK contended that the alleged business arrangement (BA) was simply a label for a supplier-customer relationship while joint venture negotiations proceeded, and that no binding joint venture agreement with the 'Capital Term' pleaded by JGJ was concluded. SRK submitted that JGJ's initial responses, including the Moses & Singer letter and its original defence, accepted liability and raised only set-off based on alleged joint venture profits, and that JGJ's later denial of any payment obligation was a belated litigation position. On appeal, SRK and The Jewelry Company argued that the High Court erred in rejecting their primary contractual claims and in treating the invoices as non-payable under an unenforceable joint venture, and that there was no basis for counter-restitution to JGJ in respect of the 42M Invoices.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The appellate court accepted SRK's position that the supply of goods to JGJ under the 23M Invoices constituted sales giving rise to contractual payment obligations. It held that JGJ's own general ledger treated the SRK and The Jewelry Company supplies as 'Purchases' and recorded payables by reference to specific invoice numbers, and that payments under the 42M Invoices were made in amounts matching invoice totals, indicating that the parties operated on a sale-and-purchase basis. The court found that JGJ's early correspondence and pleadings, including the Moses & Singer letter and its initial defence, did not deny liability but asserted set-off based on alleged joint venture profits, and that the argument that invoices were non-payable capital transfers was introduced much later without contemporaneous support. While the court agreed that there was some form of joint venture between SRK Entities and JDM Entities, it concluded that JGJ was not a party to the joint venture agreement and could not rely on its terms to avoid liability. The court rejected the notion of a 'Capital Term' under which goods supplied to JGJ were non-payable capital contributions, noting the absence of such a term in the 13 January Memo, draft JV agreements, KPMG's MIS memorandum, and other documents, and observing that even if goods were treated as capital for internal joint venture accounting, this did not extinguish JGJ's obligation to pay its suppliers. The court further held that alleged accounting reconciliation and termination settlement exercises were not proven conditions precedent to JGJ's liability and that it would be commercially implausible for SRK to supply large volumes of goods without any enforceable right to payment pending such exercises. On quantum, the court accepted evidence that the parties agreed to invoice goods on a sell-minus basis and that the invoice prices reflected that agreement. It therefore restored SRK's primary contractual claim for the full US$23,400,456.25 and The Jewelry Company's claim for US$2,211,077.91, rendering the High Court's unjust enrichment and counter-restitution analysis unnecessary. The court ordered JGJ to pay interest at 5.33% per annum on the full unpaid sums from the dates of writ and counterclaim to the date of the appellate judgment and adjusted the costs reduction applied to the SRK Parties from 25% to 15% while leaving the costs order in favour of TJC Jewelry, Inc and Ashish Shah unchanged.

Metadata.Case_Number: Civil Appeal No 22 of 2024 (Suit No 418 of 2018)
Metadata.Coram: Woo Bih Li JAD, Debbie Ong Siew Ling JAD and See Kee Oon JAD
Metadata.Tribunal_Court: Appellate Division of the High Court of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: The Jewelry Company
Party_Details.Law_Firm: Audent Chambers LLC; Oon & Bazul LLP

Facts.Text: The Jewelry Company (TJCI) is a partnership registered in India engaged in manufacturing, sales and marketing of diamond-studded jewellery; SRK owns a minority stake in TJCI through its subsidiary Ramkrishna Goldi Pvt Ltd, and TJCI is known as an affiliate of SRK.
From around April 2015, TJCI supplied jewellery and diamonds directly to JDM Entities and TJC Jewelry, Inc in the US, invoicing the goods to JGJ while the goods were physically delivered in the US.
Between 8 October 2016 and 1 August 2017, TJCI issued invoices to JGJ totalling US$2,211,077.91 for goods supplied (the '2.2M Invoices'); these invoices remained unpaid.
TJCI pleaded that it sold goods to JGJ under the 2.2M Invoices and that JGJ was obliged to pay the invoiced amounts; in the alternative, TJCI claimed reasonable compensation for the goods supplied.
On 6 November 2020, TJCI filed a counterclaim in Suit 418 against JGJ for US$2,211,077.91 under the 2.2M Invoices.

In the High Court judgment on 18 January 2024, TJCI's primary contractual claim was dismissed; TJCI succeeded on an unjust enrichment claim and was awarded US$2,027,846.91 as reasonable compensation for the goods supplied.

On 27 February 2024, the High Court ordered JGJ to pay TJCI interest at 5.33% per annum on US$2,027,846.91 from 6 November 2020 to 18 January 2024 and included TJCI in a combined costs order with SRK and certain individual defendants.
TJCI appealed jointly with SRK in Civil Appeal No 22 of 2024 against dismissal of its primary contractual claim and aspects of the costs and interest orders.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether The Jewelry Company is entitled to succeed on its primary contractual claim against JGJ for US$2,211,077.91 under the 2.2M Invoices, or only on an alternative unjust enrichment basis with a reduced quantum.
Party_Details.Rule: Invoices issued for goods supplied to a company are prima facie evidence of a contract of sale and an obligation to pay the invoiced amounts. A defendant seeking to avoid liability must prove an agreed alternative basis under which the invoices were not intended to be payable. Where a valid contract governs the supply, unjust enrichment is generally not available.
Party_Details.Application: The Jewelry Company argued that it supplied goods to JGJ under the 2.2M Invoices, that JGJ's ledgers recorded these supplies as purchases and payables, and that JGJ had not paid the invoiced amounts. It adopted SRK's submissions that there was no binding joint venture agreement with the 'Capital Term' alleged by JGJ and that JGJ's attempt to characterise the invoices as non-payable capital transfers was inconsistent with contemporaneous accounting records and payment practices. On appeal, The Jewelry Company contended that the High Court erred in treating the invoices as non-payable under an unenforceable joint venture and in limiting recovery to reasonable compensation in unjust enrichment.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The appellate court applied the same reasoning to The Jewelry Company's claim as to SRK's. It held that the 2.2M Invoices represented sales of goods to JGJ and that JGJ's own accounting records treated the supplies from The Jewelry Company as purchases with corresponding payables. The court rejected JGJ's contention that the invoices were merely mechanisms for capital transfers under a joint venture, finding no cogent evidence of an agreed term that goods supplied by The Jewelry Company to JGJ would not attract payment liability. It concluded that JGJ was contractually liable to pay the full invoiced amount of US$2,211,077.91. As with SRK's claim, the court regarded the High Court's unjust enrichment analysis as unnecessary once contractual liability was established. The court ordered JGJ to pay The Jewelry Company the full unpaid sum with interest at 5.33% per annum from 6 November 2020 to the date of the appellate judgment and left the existing costs order in favour of The Jewelry Company and Ashish Shah unchanged, save for the global adjustment to the SRK Parties' costs reduction percentage.

Metadata.Case_Number: Civil Appeal No 21 of 2024 (Suit No 418 of 2018)
Metadata.Coram: Woo Bih Li JAD, Debbie Ong Siew Ling JAD and See Kee Oon JAD
Metadata.Tribunal_Court: Appellate Division of the High Court of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Shree Ramkrishna Exports Pvt Ltd
Party_Details.Law_Firm: Audent Chambers LLC; Oon & Bazul LLP

Facts.Text: In Suit No 418 of 2018, SRK was the plaintiff and JGJ was the defendant; in Civil Appeal No 21 of 2024, SRK appeared as a respondent defending the High Court's dismissal of JGJ's counterclaims and supporting its own recovery against JGJ.

SRK opposed JGJ's appeal which sought declarations that JGJ was not subject of dispute to pay the 23M Invoices and that SRK was in breach of an alleged joint venture agreement, and resisted JGJ's attempt to revive counterclaims for breach of contract, inducement of breach, and conspiracy.

SRK argued that JGJ's conspiracy counterclaim, which sought to recover JGJ's legal costs of defending SRK's and The Jewelry Company's claims, was an improper attempt to circumvent the ordinary costs regime.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether SRK is subject of dispute on JGJ's counterclaims for breach of an alleged joint venture agreement, inducement of breach, and conspiracy to injure JGJ in relation to the issuance of the 23M and 42M Invoices and the pursuit of claims under those invoices.
Party_Details.Rule: A defendant to a counterclaim for breach of contract or inducement of breach is subject of dispute only if a binding contract with the pleaded terms exists and is enforceable by the claimant. A conspiracy to injure requires a combination between two or more persons, an intention to injure the claimant, and loss caused by the combination; legal costs of defending a claim are generally not actionable loss in conspiracy where the costs regime provides the appropriate remedy.
Party_Details.Application: SRK submitted that no enforceable joint venture agreement with the pleaded 'Capital Term' existed and that JGJ, not being a party to any such agreement, lacked standing to sue for breach. It argued that the issuance of the 23M and 42M Invoices and demands for payment were consistent with ordinary supplier-customer dealings and did not constitute breaches of any joint venture terms. On the conspiracy claim, SRK contended that JGJ's alleged loss was limited to legal fees incurred in defending SRK's and The Jewelry Company's claims, which should be addressed through costs orders rather than a tort claim, and that there was no evidence of a combination with intent to injure JGJ.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The appellate court agreed with the High Court that JGJ failed to prove the existence of an enforceable joint venture agreement with the terms it alleged and held that JGJ was not a party to any such agreement. It therefore concluded that JGJ's counterclaims for breach of contract and inducement of breach against SRK and related parties could not succeed. On the conspiracy counterclaim, the court found no evidential basis for a combination among SRK, The Jewelry Company, TJC Jewelry, Inc, and individual defendants to injure JGJ by issuing invoices and pursuing claims. It held that the only loss JGJ identified was its legal costs of defending SRK's and The Jewelry Company's claims, which are ordinarily addressed through the court's costs jurisdiction and do not constitute actionable damage in conspiracy in the circumstances. The court characterised the conspiracy counterclaim as entirely without merit and noted that it appeared retaliatory in light of parallel US proceedings. Accordingly, SRK was not liable on JGJ's counterclaims.

Metadata.Case_Number: Civil Appeal No 21 of 2024 (Suit No 418 of 2018)
Metadata.Coram: Woo Bih Li JAD, Debbie Ong Siew Ling JAD and See Kee Oon JAD
Metadata.Tribunal_Court: Appellate Division of the High Court of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: The Jewelry Company
Party_Details.Law_Firm: Audent Chambers LLC; Oon & Bazul LLP

Facts.Text: In Suit No 418 of 2018, The Jewelry Company was a defendant to JGJ's counterclaim and a counterclaimant against JGJ; in Civil Appeal No 21 of 2024, it appeared as a respondent defending dismissal of JGJ's counterclaims.
The Jewelry Company denied that it was party to any joint venture agreement with the terms alleged by JGJ and denied involvement in any conspiracy to injure JGJ.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether The Jewelry Company is subject of dispute on JGJ's counterclaims for breach of an alleged joint venture agreement, inducement of breach, and conspiracy to injure JGJ in relation to the 23M and 42M Invoices and related payment flows.
Party_Details.Rule: Liability for breach of contract or inducement of breach requires a binding contract enforceable by the claimant and conduct amounting to breach or inducement. Conspiracy to injure requires a combination, intent to injure, unlawful means or predominant purpose to injure, and actionable loss.
Party_Details.Application: The Jewelry Company argued that JGJ had not established any enforceable joint venture agreement to which The Jewelry Company was bound, and that its role was to supply goods to JGJ under invoices. It submitted that there was no evidence that it participated in any plan to manipulate invoice prices or to cause JGJ to pay sums it was not subject of dispute to pay, and that JGJ's alleged loss was confined to legal costs, which should be dealt with through costs orders rather than a tort claim.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The appellate court held that JGJ's counterclaims against The Jewelry Company failed for the same reasons as its claims against SRK. JGJ did not prove an enforceable joint venture agreement with the pleaded terms, and JGJ was not a party to any such agreement. The court found no evidence that The Jewelry Company induced any breach of a joint venture agreement or participated in a conspiracy to injure JGJ. It reiterated that JGJ's attempt to recover its defence costs through a conspiracy claim was an improper use of the tort and that the appropriate mechanism for allocating legal costs is the court's costs jurisdiction. Accordingly, The Jewelry Company was not liable on JGJ's counterclaims.

Metadata.Case_Number: Civil Appeal No 21 of 2024 (Suit No 418 of 2018)
Metadata.Coram: Woo Bih Li JAD, Debbie Ong Siew Ling JAD and See Kee Oon JAD
Metadata.Tribunal_Court: Appellate Division of the High Court of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: TJC Jewelry, Inc

Facts.Text: TJC Jewelry, Inc (TJCNY) is a US-incorporated company in the business of sales and marketing of jewellery and acts as the marketing affiliate of The Jewelry Company, helping to market its products in the US.
TJCNY was named as a defendant to JGJ's counterclaim in Suit 418, accused of inducing breaches of an alleged joint venture agreement and conspiring with SRK Entities and individuals to injure JGJ.
On 28 December 2017, TJCNY commenced proceedings in New York (Case Index No. 657583/2017) against JDM Entities to recover diamonds and jewellery inventory allegedly stored in JDM's New York vault.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether TJC Jewelry, Inc is subject of dispute on JGJ's counterclaims for inducing breaches of an alleged joint venture agreement and conspiring to injure JGJ in connection with invoice pricing, payment flows, and litigation strategy.
Party_Details.Rule: A claim for inducing breach of contract requires proof of a valid contract, knowledge of the contract, an intentional act to procure its breach, and resulting damage. Conspiracy to injure requires a combination, intent to injure, and actionable loss. Absent an enforceable underlying contract and evidence of concerted action with intent to injure, such claims fail.
Party_Details.Application: TJCNY argued that it was not a party to any joint venture agreement and that its role was limited to marketing and administrative assistance in the US. It denied involvement in setting invoice prices or instructing JGJ's bank to make payments and contended that JGJ had not shown any acts by TJCNY that induced breaches of a joint venture agreement or formed part of a conspiracy to injure JGJ. TJCNY also submitted that JGJ's alleged loss was limited to legal costs, which should be addressed through costs orders.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The appellate court held that JGJ's counterclaims against TJC Jewelry, Inc could not succeed because JGJ failed to establish an enforceable joint venture agreement or its own standing to enforce such an agreement. The court found no evidence that TJCNY induced any breach of a joint venture agreement or participated in a conspiracy to injure JGJ. It noted that TJCNY's role was primarily to market products in the US and that there was no proof that it directed invoice pricing or payment approvals in a way that breached contractual obligations owed to JGJ. The court reiterated that JGJ's attempt to recover its defence costs through a conspiracy claim was misconceived. Accordingly, TJCNY was not liable on JGJ's counterclaims, and the costs order in its favour was left intact.

Metadata.Case_Number: Civil Appeal No 21 of 2024 (Suit No 418 of 2018)
Metadata.Coram: Woo Bih Li JAD, Debbie Ong Siew Ling JAD and See Kee Oon JAD
Metadata.Tribunal_Court: Appellate Division of the High Court of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Govind Dholakia
Party_Details.Law_Firm: Audent Chambers LLC; Oon & Bazul LLP

Facts.Text: Govind Dholakia is an Indian national, founder, chairman and director of SRK.
Govind was named as a defendant to JGJ's counterclaim in Suit 418, accused of participating in alleged breaches of a joint venture agreement and conspiracy to injure JGJ.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether Govind Dholakia is personally subject of dispute on JGJ's counterclaims for breach of an alleged joint venture agreement, inducement of breach, and conspiracy to injure JGJ.
Party_Details.Rule: Personal liability for inducing breach of contract or conspiracy requires proof of a valid and enforceable contract, intentional acts by the individual to procure breach or to combine with others to injure, and resulting loss. Where the underlying contract is not established and the alleged loss is limited to legal costs, such claims fail.
Party_Details.Application: Govind, through counsel, adopted the SRK Parties' submissions that no enforceable joint venture agreement with the pleaded terms existed, that JGJ was not a party to any such agreement, and that there was no evidence of a combination or intent to injure JGJ. He argued that his involvement was in his capacity as a senior officer of SRK and that JGJ's remedy, if any, lay in costs orders rather than tort claims.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The appellate court held that JGJ's counterclaims against Govind Dholakia failed because JGJ did not prove an enforceable joint venture agreement or its own standing to enforce such an agreement. The court found no specific evidence that Govind personally procured breaches of contractual obligations owed to JGJ or entered into a combination with others with the predominant purpose of injuring JGJ. It reiterated that JGJ's alleged loss, being its legal costs of defending claims, was not actionable damage in conspiracy in the circumstances. Accordingly, Govind was not personally liable on JGJ's counterclaims.

Metadata.Case_Number: Civil Appeal No 21 of 2024 (Suit No 418 of 2018)
Metadata.Coram: Woo Bih Li JAD, Debbie Ong Siew Ling JAD and See Kee Oon JAD
Metadata.Tribunal_Court: Appellate Division of the High Court of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Rahul Dholakia
Party_Details.Law_Firm: Audent Chambers LLC; Oon & Bazul LLP

Facts.Text: Rahul Dholakia is an Indian national, Managing Director of SRK and a partner of The Jewelry Company; he is Govind Dholakia's nephew.
Rahul was named as a defendant to JGJ's counterclaim in Suit 418, accused of involvement in alleged breaches of a joint venture agreement and conspiracy to injure JGJ.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether Rahul Dholakia is personally subject of dispute on JGJ's counterclaims for breach of an alleged joint venture agreement, inducement of breach, and conspiracy to injure JGJ.
Party_Details.Rule: Individual liability for inducing breach of contract or conspiracy requires proof of an enforceable contract, intentional acts to procure breach or to combine to injure, and actionable loss. Absent these elements, personal liability does not arise.
Party_Details.Application: Rahul, through counsel, adopted the SRK Parties' submissions that no enforceable joint venture agreement with the pleaded terms existed, that JGJ was not a party to any such agreement, and that there was no evidence of a combination or predominant intention to injure JGJ. He argued that his conduct in discussions about the collaboration and its termination did not amount to tortious wrongdoing against JGJ.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The appellate court concluded that JGJ's counterclaims against Rahul Dholakia failed for the same reasons as its claims against SRK and other individual defendants. JGJ did not establish an enforceable joint venture agreement or its own standing to enforce such an agreement, and there was no evidence that Rahul personally induced breaches of contractual obligations owed to JGJ or participated in a conspiracy to injure JGJ. The court reiterated that JGJ's alleged loss was confined to legal costs, which are governed by the costs regime. Accordingly, Rahul was not liable on JGJ's counterclaims.

Metadata.Case_Number: Civil Appeal No 21 of 2024 (Suit No 418 of 2018)
Metadata.Coram: Woo Bih Li JAD, Debbie Ong Siew Ling JAD and See Kee Oon JAD
Metadata.Tribunal_Court: Appellate Division of the High Court of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Nirav Narola
Party_Details.Law_Firm: Audent Chambers LLC; Oon & Bazul LLP

Facts.Text: Nirav Narola is an Indian national, an employee of SRK and a partner of The Jewelry Company; he is Govind Dholakia's grandson and Rahul Dholakia's nephew.
Nirav was named as a defendant to JGJ's counterclaim in Suit 418, accused of involvement in alleged breaches of a joint venture agreement and conspiracy to injure JGJ.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether Nirav Narola is personally subject of dispute on JGJ's counterclaims for breach of an alleged joint venture agreement, inducement of breach, and conspiracy to injure JGJ.
Party_Details.Rule: Personal liability for inducing breach of contract or conspiracy requires proof of an enforceable contract, intentional acts to procure breach or to combine to injure, and actionable loss. Without these, the claim fails.
Party_Details.Application: Nirav, through counsel, adopted the SRK Parties' submissions that no enforceable joint venture agreement with the pleaded terms existed, that JGJ was not a party to any such agreement, and that there was no evidence of a combination or intent to injure JGJ. He argued that his role in back-office coordination and communications did not amount to tortious conduct against JGJ.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The appellate court held that JGJ's counterclaims against Nirav Narola failed because JGJ did not prove an enforceable joint venture agreement or its own standing to enforce such an agreement, and there was no evidence that Nirav personally induced breaches of contractual obligations owed to JGJ or participated in a conspiracy to injure JGJ. The court noted that Nirav's testimony about certain spreadsheets did not support JGJ's theory that JDM, rather than JGJ, was liable for the goods, and that JGJ's alleged loss was limited to legal costs. Accordingly, Nirav was not liable on JGJ's counterclaims.

Metadata.Case_Number: Civil Appeal No 21 of 2024 (Suit No 418 of 2018)
Metadata.Coram: Woo Bih Li JAD, Debbie Ong Siew Ling JAD and See Kee Oon JAD
Metadata.Tribunal_Court: Appellate Division of the High Court of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Amit Shah
Party_Details.Law_Firm: Audent Chambers LLC; Oon & Bazul LLP

Facts.Text: Amit Shah is an Indian national, Chief Executive Officer and partner of The Jewelry Company.

Amit was named as a defendant to JGJ's counterclaim in Suit 418, accused of misusing access to JGJ's IDBNY account to cause payments of US$42,994,312.66 to SRK under the 42M Invoices and of participating in alleged breaches of a joint venture agreement and conspiracy.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether Amit Shah is personally subject of dispute on JGJ's counterclaims for breach of an alleged joint venture agreement, inducing breach, unjust enrichment, and conspiracy to injure JGJ in relation to payments under the 42M Invoices and related conduct.
Party_Details.Rule: A claim for unjust enrichment against a recipient of payments requires proof that the payments were not due under a valid contract and that the recipient was enriched at the claimant's expense. Personal liability for inducing breach or conspiracy requires an enforceable contract, intentional acts to procure breach or to combine to injure, and actionable loss.
Party_Details.Application: Amit, through counsel, argued that payments from JGJ's IDBNY account to SRK under the 42M Invoices were made pursuant to agreed mechanisms for settling invoices and that JGJ was contractually subject of dispute for those invoices. He denied any misuse of his signatory authority and contended that JGJ's unjust enrichment and conspiracy claims were premised on an unproven joint venture agreement and an incorrect characterisation of the payments as capital transfers rather than invoice settlements.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The appellate court held that JGJ was contractually liable to pay SRK the full amounts under the 42M Invoices and that there was no basis for JGJ's counter-restitution claim. It found that payments authorised from JGJ's IDBNY account, including those approved by Amit Shah, were consistent with the agreed process for paying supplier invoices and did not constitute wrongful misappropriation. Because JGJ failed to establish an enforceable joint venture agreement or its own standing to enforce such an agreement, its claims for breach, inducement of breach, and conspiracy against Amit could not succeed. The court also rejected JGJ's attempt to treat its defence costs as actionable loss in conspiracy. Accordingly, Amit was not personally liable on JGJ's counterclaims.

Metadata.Case_Number: Civil Appeal No 21 of 2024 (Suit No 418 of 2018)
Metadata.Coram: Woo Bih Li JAD, Debbie Ong Siew Ling JAD and See Kee Oon JAD
Metadata.Tribunal_Court: Appellate Division of the High Court of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Ashish Shah

Facts.Text: Ashish Shah is a US national, sole shareholder and president of TJC Jewelry, Inc.

Ashish was named as a defendant to JGJ's counterclaim in Suit 418, accused of misusing access to JGJ's IDBNY account to approve payments to SRK under the 42M Invoices and of participating in alleged breaches of a joint venture agreement and conspiracy.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether Ashish Shah is personally subject of dispute on JGJ's counterclaims for breach of an alleged joint venture agreement, inducing breach, unjust enrichment, and conspiracy to injure JGJ in relation to payments under the 42M Invoices and related conduct.
Party_Details.Rule: Personal liability for unjust enrichment, inducing breach, or conspiracy requires proof that payments were not due under a valid contract, that the individual intentionally procured breaches or combined to injure, and that the claimant suffered actionable loss. Where the underlying invoices are contractually payable, counter-restitution and conspiracy claims fail.
Party_Details.Application: Ashish, through counsel, argued that his role as an authorised signatory on JGJ's IDBNY account was to facilitate agreed payment processes and that approvals of payments to SRK were consistent with JGJ's obligations under the 42M Invoices. He denied any wrongful intent or misuse of authority and contended that JGJ's claims were premised on an unproven joint venture agreement and an incorrect assertion that the invoices were non-payable capital transfers.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The appellate court held that JGJ was contractually liable for the 42M Invoices and that payments approved by Ashish Shah from JGJ's IDBNY account were made in discharge of those obligations. It found no basis for JGJ's unjust enrichment claim seeking return of the entire US$42,994,312.66, characterising the attempt as an effort to retain goods without paying for them. Because JGJ failed to establish an enforceable joint venture agreement or its own standing to enforce such an agreement, its claims for breach, inducement of breach, and conspiracy against Ashish could not succeed. The court reiterated that JGJ's alleged loss was limited to legal costs, which are governed by the costs regime. Accordingly, Ashish was not personally liable on JGJ's counterclaims, and the costs order in his favour was maintained.

Metadata.Case_Number: Civil Appeal No 23 of 2024 (Suit No 475 of 2018)
Metadata.Coram: Woo Bih Li JAD, Debbie Ong Siew Ling JAD and See Kee Oon JAD
Metadata.Tribunal_Court: Appellate Division of the High Court of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Michael Bernard Kriss

Facts.Text: Michael Bernard Kriss is a US national, a director of JGJ and holder of 25% of JGJ's shares; he is one of the 'Kriss Brothers' who own and control JDM Entities in the US.
On 22 January 2018, Michael signed the First Resolution authorising JGJ to commence legal proceedings against TJC Jewelry, Inc to recover US$3,733,503.43.
On 15 February 2018, Michael signed the Second Resolution authorising JGJ to commence legal proceedings against SRK and The Jewelry Company to recover alleged overcharges.
On 6 March 2018, Michael signed the Third Resolution approving JGJ's 2016 revised financial statements, which included four adjustments, and resolving to convene the AGM.

On 12 January 2018, Michael sent JGJ's auditors AT Adler a copy of JGJ's 2016 revised financial statements, explaining that the initial financial statements did not correctly reflect JGJ's financial position.

Michael and other JDM representatives caused four adjustments to be made to JGJ's 2016 financial statements: a commission rebate expense, a reduction of purchases-finished goods by US$7,540,283, an addition of US$15,488,893 due from SRK, and additions of US$7,744,446.50 each due to Michael and David as capital repayments.

In Suit No 475 of 2018, Michael defended against Shailesh's minority oppression claim and, together with JGJ, counterclaimed that Shailesh breached his management obligations by refusing to sign or abstain on the First, Second and Third Resolutions and by requesting information for alleged collateral purposes.
On 18 January 2024, the High Court allowed Shailesh's oppression claim in part based on the Three Adjustments and dismissed JGJ's counterclaim; Michael appealed in Civil Appeal No 23 of 2024.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether the Three Adjustments to JGJ's 2016 revised financial statements constituted oppressive conduct towards Shailesh as a minority shareholder under s 216 of the Companies Act, and whether the High Court erred in granting relief requiring restatement of JGJ's financial statements.
Party_Details.Rule: A claim under s 216 of the Companies Act requires proof that the affairs of the company have been conducted in a manner oppressive to, or in disregard of, the interests of the complainant as a shareholder. The court distinguishes between corporate wrongs and personal wrongs; where the alleged wrong is a corporate wrong and the shareholder's loss is merely reflective of the company's loss, s 216 is not the appropriate remedy. The nature of the injury and the remedy sought are central to this analysis.
Party_Details.Application: Michael argued on appeal that Shailesh was a nominee shareholder for SRK and could not assert personal legitimate expectations based on directors' duties. He submitted that any issues with the Three Adjustments were corporate wrongs affecting JGJ and that Shailesh's alleged loss was merely reflective of the company's position. He contended that the adjustments were management decisions aimed at correcting perceived inaccuracies and that any failure to maintain true and fair accounts should be addressed through corporate remedies, not oppression relief. He also argued that Shailesh had an exit mechanism under JGJ's Articles which he did not invoke.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The appellate court agreed with the High Court that Shailesh held his shares in JGJ as SRK's nominee but held that this did not bar him from bringing an oppression claim as the registered shareholder. However, the court emphasised that Shailesh's pleaded case did not rely on his nominee status and that SRK's interests could not be taken into account in assessing oppression. The court examined the Three Adjustments and accepted that they were made in an arbitrary and unsubstantiated manner, noting that the accounts payable reduction and the SRK payable and capital repayment adjustments lacked a clear basis and were described by JDM's CFO as 'convenient numbers'. Nonetheless, the court held that the real injury caused by the adjustments was to JGJ as a company, not to Shailesh personally, and that any diminution in the value of Shailesh's shares was merely reflective of the company's position. The relief sought—restatement of JGJ's financial statements—was also a corporate remedy. Applying the principles in Suying Design and Ng Kek Wee, the court concluded that the Three Adjustments constituted corporate wrongs and that s 216 should not be used to vindicate such wrongs. It therefore allowed Michael's appeal, set aside the oppression finding based on the Three Adjustments, and dismissed Shailesh's s 216 claim in its entirety, leaving it to the directors to decide whether to reverse the adjustments voluntarily.

Metadata.Case_Number: Civil Appeal No 23 of 2024 (Suit No 475 of 2018)
Metadata.Coram: Woo Bih Li JAD, Debbie Ong Siew Ling JAD and See Kee Oon JAD
Metadata.Tribunal_Court: Appellate Division of the High Court of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: David Miles Kriss

Facts.Text: David Miles Kriss is a US national, a director of JGJ and holder of 25% of JGJ's shares; he is one of the 'Kriss Brothers' who own and control JDM Entities in the US.
On 22 January 2018, David signed the First Resolution authorising JGJ to commence legal proceedings against TJC Jewelry, Inc to recover US$3,733,503.43.
On 15 February 2018, David signed the Second Resolution authorising JGJ to commence legal proceedings against SRK and The Jewelry Company to recover alleged overcharges.
On 6 March 2018, David signed the Third Resolution approving JGJ's 2016 revised financial statements, including the Three Adjustments, and resolving to convene the AGM.
The 2016 revised financial statements recorded two equal amounts of US$7,744,446.50 each as 'Due to Michael Kriss' and 'Due to David Kriss' as part of a capital repayment adjustment.

In Suit No 475 of 2018, David defended against Shailesh's minority oppression claim and, together with JGJ, counterclaimed that Shailesh breached his management obligations in relation to the Three Resolutions and information requests.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether the Three Adjustments to JGJ's 2016 revised financial statements constituted oppressive conduct towards Shailesh as a minority shareholder under s 216 of the Companies Act, and whether the High Court erred in granting relief requiring restatement of JGJ's financial statements.
Party_Details.Rule: Oppression under s 216 requires conduct that is commercially unfair to the complainant as a shareholder and causes personal injury distinct from the company's loss. Corporate mismanagement or accounting irregularities that primarily injure the company are generally to be addressed through corporate remedies or derivative actions.
Party_Details.Application: David adopted Michael's submissions that Shailesh, as SRK's nominee, could not show personal injury distinct from JGJ's and that the Three Adjustments, even if unjustified, were corporate matters. He argued that the adjustments were intended to correct perceived overbilling and capital imbalances and that any issues with them should be addressed through corporate processes rather than oppression relief.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: For the reasons given in relation to Michael's appeal, the appellate court held that the Three Adjustments, while unjustified, did not amount to oppression of Shailesh's interests as a shareholder under s 216. The court found that any injury arising from the adjustments was suffered by JGJ as a company and that Shailesh's loss, if any, was merely reflective. It therefore allowed David's appeal, set aside the High Court's order requiring restatement of JGJ's financial statements, and dismissed Shailesh's oppression claim in its entirety.

Metadata.Case_Number: Civil Appeal No 24 of 2024 (Suit No 475 of 2018)
Metadata.Coram: Woo Bih Li JAD, Debbie Ong Siew Ling JAD and See Kee Oon JAD
Metadata.Tribunal_Court: Appellate Division of the High Court of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: J. G. Jewelry Pte Ltd

Facts.Text: On 30 May 2018, JGJ filed a defence and counterclaim in Suit No 475 of 2018 alleging that Shailesh breached his management obligations by refusing or failing to sign the First, Second and Third Resolutions and by requesting information and documents for alleged collateral purposes.

JGJ sought declarations that Shailesh had breached his management obligations, an injunction restraining him from seeking information or participating in decisions relating to JGJ's claims against SRK Entities, and damages in respect of legal fees incurred in responding to his solicitors' letters.
On 18 January 2024, the High Court dismissed JGJ's counterclaim against Shailesh, finding no breach of management obligations in relation to the Three Resolutions or information requests.

JGJ appealed in Civil Appeal No 24 of 2024, arguing that as SRK's nominee director, Shailesh should have abstained from voting on the Three Resolutions and that his conduct engaged in disputed conduct to JGJ.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether Shailesh breached his management obligations as a director of JGJ by refusing to sign or abstain on the First, Second and Third Resolutions and by requesting information and documents, and whether JGJ is entitled to an injunction and damages for legal fees.
Party_Details.Rule: A director owes management obligations to act bona fide in the best interests of the company and to avoid conflicts of interest. A nominee director may consider the interests of his nominator so long as they do not conflict with the company's interests. The court assesses breach by asking whether no reasonable director would have considered the impugned conduct to be in the company's interests. Injunctions and damages are discretionary remedies and will not be granted where they serve no useful purpose or are disproportionate.
Party_Details.Application: JGJ argued that Shailesh, as SRK's nominee, was in a position of conflict regarding resolutions authorising litigation against SRK Entities and should have abstained from voting. It contended that his refusal to sign the First and Second Resolutions and his objections to the Third Resolution, coupled with information requests, were aimed at protecting SRK's interests and frustrating JGJ's efforts to pursue claims and comply with statutory filing obligations. JGJ sought an injunction to restrain Shailesh from seeking information or participating in decisions relating to claims against SRK Entities and damages for legal fees incurred in responding to his solicitors' letters.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The appellate court agreed with the High Court that Shailesh was SRK's nominee director but held that this did not automatically render his conduct a breach of duty. It noted that the First and Second Resolutions were presented to Shailesh on short timelines and that JGJ, through its CFO, sought his signature without indicating that he should abstain. The court found that Shailesh's objections and requests for information, including concerns about selective pursuit of debts and allegations of overcharging, were not so unreasonable that no director could regard them as in JGJ's interests, particularly in the context of a breakdown in the joint venture and competing claims between SRK Entities and JDM Entities. Regarding the Third Resolution, the court observed that Shailesh's request to postpone the AGM until he received information about the 2016 revised financial statements and the auditors' concerns was linked to his doubts about the Three Adjustments, which the court itself found unjustified. It held that Shailesh's stance did not amount to a breach of fiduciary duty. On remedies, the court considered that JGJ had already declined to provide the requested information and proceeded with the resolutions, and that there was no ongoing conduct warranting an injunction. It also viewed the claimed damages of about S$19,655.50 in legal fees as insufficient to justify the counterclaim, which appeared retaliatory. The court therefore dismissed JGJ's appeal, refused the injunction and damages, and ordered that parties bear their own costs in Suit 475 and in Civil Appeals Nos 23 and 24 of 2024.

Metadata.Case_Number: Civil Appeal No 23 of 2024 (Suit No 475 of 2018)
Metadata.Coram: Woo Bih Li JAD, Debbie Ong Siew Ling JAD and See Kee Oon JAD
Metadata.Tribunal_Court: Appellate Division of the High Court of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Shaileshkumar Manubhai Khunt

Facts.Text: Shaileshkumar Manubhai Khunt (Shailesh) is an Indian national resident in Hong Kong, a director and 50% shareholder of JGJ; he previously worked for SRK and is a director of S Goldi (Asia) Limited, SRK's marketing arm in Hong Kong.
The appellate court affirmed that Shailesh held his shares in JGJ as SRK's nominee shareholder and director.

On 26 and 27 January 2018, Shailesh received emails from JDM CFO Jim Goldsborough attaching the First Resolution for his signature; he did not sign and later, through solicitors, objected to the resolution.

On 6 February 2018, Shailesh's solicitors sent a letter to the Kriss Brothers objecting to the First Resolution on grounds including insufficient information, lack of discussion, and selective pursuit of debts.
On 19 February 2018, Shailesh received the Second Resolution for signature; he did not sign and his solicitors later denied allegations of price manipulation by SRK and The Jewelry Company.

On 16 April 2018, Shailesh emailed JGJ's corporate secretary objecting to the AGM scheduled for 19 April 2018 and requested postponement until he received information and documents relating to the 2016 revised financial statements.

On 16 April 2018, Shailesh emailed JGJ's auditors AT Adler expressing concern about material variations in JGJ's 2016 revised financial statements, listing the four adjustments, and requesting bank account details, management accounts, and financial statements.

On 4 May 2018, Shailesh commenced Suit No 475 of 2018 under s 216 of the Companies Act against the Kriss Brothers and JGJ, alleging oppressive conduct including the Three Resolutions and the Four Adjustments.

On 18 January 2024, the High Court allowed Shailesh's oppression claim in part based on the Three Adjustments and dismissed JGJ's counterclaim; Shailesh did not appeal that decision but resisted the Kriss Brothers' and JGJ's appeals.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether Shailesh succeeded on his minority oppression claim under s 216 of the Companies Act in respect of the Three Adjustments to JGJ's 2016 revised financial statements, and whether he breached his management obligations as a director in relation to the Three Resolutions and information requests.
Party_Details.Rule: For oppression, the court examines whether the company's affairs were conducted in a manner commercially unfair to the complainant as a shareholder, causing personal injury distinct from the company's loss. For directors' duties, a nominee director must act bona fide in the company's interests and may consider the nominator's interests where they do not conflict; breach is assessed by whether no reasonable director would have acted similarly.
Party_Details.Application: Shailesh argued that the Kriss Brothers' passage of the First and Second Resolutions without adequate information and their making of the Four Adjustments, particularly the Three Adjustments, were commercially unfair to him as a shareholder and engaged in disputed conduct to ensure true and fair accounts. He sought orders requiring restatement of JGJ's financial statements and measures to address alleged preferential treatment of JDM Entities. In response to JGJ's counterclaim, he contended that his refusal to sign the Three Resolutions and his information requests were motivated by concern for JGJ's interests and the accuracy of its accounts, and that he did not act in breach of his management obligations.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The appellate court held that Shailesh's oppression claim ultimately failed because the Three Adjustments, while unjustified, constituted corporate wrongs affecting JGJ rather than personal wrongs to Shailesh. It found that any injury from inaccurate accounts was suffered by the company and that Shailesh's loss was merely reflective. The court therefore set aside the High Court's oppression finding and the order requiring restatement of JGJ's financial statements. On the counterclaim, the court agreed with the High Court that Shailesh did not breach his fiduciary duties in relation to the Three Resolutions or his information requests. It noted that the resolutions were presented on short notice, that JGJ itself sought his signature without suggesting abstention, and that his objections and requests were linked to legitimate concerns about selective litigation and the unexplained Three Adjustments. The court held that Shailesh's conduct was not so unreasonable that no director could regard it as in JGJ's interests. It declined to grant JGJ's requested injunction and damages and ordered that each side bear its own costs in Suit 475 and in the appeals.

Defendant: Shree Ramkrishna Exports Pvt Ltd, The Jewelry Company, TJC Jewelry, Inc, Govind Dholakia, Rahul Dholakia, Nirav Narola, Amit Shah, Ashish Shah
